label: Newsletter Issue
description: A full newsletter issue with subject lines and structure

fields.label: Issue topic & key points
fields.placeholder: Why most onboarding emails fail; the 3-email minimum; a teardown of a good flow.

fields.label: Primary CTA
fields.placeholder: Reply with your biggest onboarding blocker

label: Drip / Nurture Sequence
description: Multi-email nurture sequence toward one conversion

fields.label: Product, audience & the goal
fields.placeholder: B2B analytics tool. Audience: trial users who haven't connected data. Goal: get them to first insight, then upgrade.

template:
You are a lifecycle email strategist and copywriter. Design a {{emailCount}}-email {{trigger}} nurture sequence: "{{sequenceName}}".

## Context
{{context}}

- Audience: {{audience}}
- Tone: {{tone}}
{{#if cta}}- Final conversion action: {{cta}}
{{/if}}
## Requirements
- Map the sequence arc: each email's single job, the objection/stage it addresses, and how it moves the reader forward (not just "check in").
- For every email provide: send timing/trigger, goal, 2 subject-line options + preheader, full body copy, and one clear CTA.
- Escalate intent gradually: educate and create value before asking for the conversion.
{{#if branching}}- Add behavior-based branching (engaged vs inactive, clicked vs not, converted -> exit) with the alternate path logic.
{{/if}}- Keep emails short, scannable, and human; no manipulative urgency.

## Deliverables
1. A sequence map (email, timing, goal, CTA{{#if branching}}, branch{{/if}}).
2. Full copy for every email with subject + preheader options.
3. Suggested success metric per email and the overall conversion KPI.

Make well-reasoned assumptions about timing and product details, label them, and proceed.

label: User Onboarding Flow
description: Activation-focused onboarding email flow to first value

fields.label: What it does & the activation moment
fields.placeholder: Product analytics tool. 'Aha' = seeing a funnel after connecting a data source within 7 days.

fields.label: Activation goal
fields.placeholder: Connect a data source and view first funnel

label: Re-engagement Campaign
description: Win-back emails for inactive or churned users

fields.label: Who's inactive & why (if known)
fields.placeholder: Trial users who connected data but never returned after week 1; likely never hit a clear insight.

fields.label: Incentive / hook
fields.placeholder: Extended trial, new feature, or just a useful insight

fields.label: Include a final break-up / sunset email

template:
You are a retention marketer specializing in win-back. Create a {{emailCount}}-email re-engagement campaign for "{{brand}}" targeting the {{inactivityType}} segment.

## Context
{{context}}

- Audience: {{audience}}
- Tone: {{tone}}
{{#if incentive}}- Incentive/hook available: {{incentive}}
{{/if}}
## Requirements
- Lead with the reason they left, not a guilt trip; reframe around the value they're missing.
- Vary the angle across emails: reminder of value, new reason to return / what's changed, and a low-friction win or incentive.
- For each email: timing, objective, 2 subject-line options + preheader, body, and one clear CTA (make returning effortless).
{{#if sunset}}- End with a respectful sunset/break-up email that asks for a final decision and protects deliverability (offer to reduce frequency or unsubscribe cleanly).
{{/if}}- Keep it short and respectful; no dark patterns or fake urgency.

## Deliverables
1. The campaign map (email, timing, angle, CTA).
2. Full copy with subject + preheader options.
3. Suppression/exit logic and the metric that defines success.

Make sensible assumptions about the segment, label them, and proceed.

label: Cold Outreach Email
description: Personalized B2B cold email with follow-ups

fields.label: What you're offering
fields.placeholder: We help B2B SaaS cut onboarding time ~40% with done-for-you lifecycle email.

fields.label: Who you're targeting (ICP)
fields.placeholder: Heads of Growth at 20-200 person B2B SaaS companies with a free trial.

fields.label: Personalization angle
fields.placeholder: Just raised a Series A; hiring growth roles; competitor recently launched onboarding

options.label: Get them to take a resource

template:
You are a top B2B cold-email copywriter who gets replies, not spam complaints. Write a cold outreach email plus follow-ups.

## Offer
{{offer}}

## Target (ICP)
{{targetProfile}}
{{#if trigger}}
- Personalization angle to lead with: {{trigger}}
{{/if}}- Goal of the sequence: {{goal}}
- Tone: {{tone}}

## Requirements
- Keep the first email short (under ~90 words): a relevant opener (about them, not you), a one-line credible value claim with proof, and one soft, specific ask.
- No "I hope this finds you well", no feature dumps, no fake flattery. Write like a busy human to a busy human.
{{#if subjectVariants}}- Provide 3 subject-line options (short, lowercase-ok, curiosity/relevance, no clickbait).
{{else}}- Provide one strong, low-key subject line.
{{/if}}- Then write {{followups}} follow-ups, each adding a NEW angle/value (case study, insight, different ask), never just "bumping this". Include send-day spacing.
- Make the CTA frictionless and aligned to: {{goal}}.

## Deliverables
1. {{#if subjectVariants}}Subject-line options.
{{/if}}2. Email 1 body, then each follow-up with timing and its distinct angle.
3. One personalization checklist the rep must complete before sending.

Make confident copy choices; only ask if the offer or ICP is unclear.

label: Product Announcement
description: Launch email for a new product, feature or update

fields.label: What it is & why it matters
fields.placeholder: Compare funnels across segments side by side. Solves the #1 requested workflow; saves analysts hours.

template:
You are a product marketing copywriter. Write a {{announcementType}} announcement email for "{{productName}}".

## What's launching
{{details}}

- Audience: {{audience}}
- Tone: {{tone}}
{{#if cta}}- Primary CTA: {{cta}}
{{/if}}
## Requirements
- Lead with the customer outcome/benefit, not the feature name or internal excitement.
- Structure: hook -> the problem it solves -> what it does (briefly) -> proof or example -> one clear CTA.
- Provide {{subjectCount}} subject-line options + a preheader for each (benefit-led, no hype words that trip spam filters).
{{#if announcementType == "Change / deprecation"}}- Be transparent and specific about what changes, the timeline, the impact, and exactly what the user must do; lead with empathy.
{{/if}}{{#if segmented}}- Provide tailored body variants for distinct segments (e.g. power users vs new users) noting what differs and why.
{{/if}}- Keep it tight and scannable; respect the reader's time.

## Deliverables
1. Subject-line + preheader options.
2. The full email body{{#if segmented}} plus segment variants{{/if}}.
3. A suggested in-product or follow-up touch to pair with the email.

Make confident messaging choices; only ask if the launch details are ambiguous.

label: Subject Line Set
description: A/B-ready subject lines and preheaders for one email

fields.label: Email purpose & key message
fields.placeholder: Promo: 20% off annual plans this week for trial users who haven't upgraded.

template:
You are an email copywriter who lives and dies by open rates. Generate a subject-line test set for a {{emailType}} email.

## Email purpose
{{emailPurpose}}

- Audience: {{audience}}
- Tone: {{tone}}
- Angles to cover: {{angles}}

## Requirements
- Write {{count}} subject lines spread deliberately across the requested angles (label each line with its angle).
- Keep them mostly under ~50 characters / mobile-safe; pair each with a complementary preheader (don't repeat the subject).
- Avoid spam-trigger words and ALL CAPS; no clickbait that the email can't pay off.
{{#if emoji}}- Emoji allowed where it genuinely adds clarity or emotion; never more than one, never decorative.
{{else}}- No emoji.
{{/if}}- Ensure variety in length and structure so a real A/B/n test is meaningful.

## Deliverables
1. The numbered subject + preheader pairs, each tagged with its angle and character count.
2. A recommended top 3 to test first, with a one-line rationale each.
3. One note on what a win on each would tell you about the audience.

Make confident copy choices; only ask if the email's core promise is unclear.
